title: Hospital beds (per 1,000 people)
definition: Hospital beds include inpatient beds available in public, private, general, and specialized hospitals and rehabilitation centers. In most cases beds for both acute and chronic care are included.

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: WHO data, supplemented by country data, World Health Organization (WHO)

editorial.shortHook: A health-capacity map with post-Soviet and rich-country clues.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Korea, Rep., Japan, Belarus, while the lightest include Chad, Burkina Faso, Niger; that contrast is the fastest way to read the hospital beds pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Hospital beds reveal infrastructure choices that do not always match doctor density or spending.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Birth rate can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Physicians can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Maternal mortality can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
regionalSignals: Health maps often separate income, public-health capacity, and conflict exposure.
Check both high-income and low-income anchors.

reviewNotes: Distinctive expert map with health-system legacy patterns.
Too easy to overread as health quality, so keep it out of default Daily.
